{{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[Category:Pages In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) {{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[category:Pages no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) revision.text:
{{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Nakatomi clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:1 revision imported]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:Shinbetsu]] [[category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Nakatomi clan]][[category:Shinbetsu]] {{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[category:kk]] [[category:Aristocracy of Classical However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} {{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe {{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe revision.text:
{{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakatomi clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) {{Short description|Ancient Japanese aristocratic clan group}} {{family name hatnote|Nakatomi|lang=Japanese}}{{Lead too short|date=May 2024}}{{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Nakatomi |surname nihongo = 中臣 |titles =Various |cadet branches =[[Fujiwara clan]]
[[Ōnakatomi clan]] | home province = | founder = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | final ruler = [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Nakatomi no Itohito]] | founding year = Unknown }} {{nihongo|'''Nakatomi clan'''|中臣氏|Nakatomi-uji}} was a [[Japan|Japanese]] aristocratic kin group (''[[Uji (clan)|uji]]'').[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Nakatomi," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 39]; retrieved 2013-5-5. The clan claims descent from [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane]].{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2020-11-20|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} ==History== The Nakatomi was an influential clan in [[Classical Japan]]. Along with the [[Inbe clan]], the Nakatomi were one of the two clans that oversaw certain important national rites, and one of many to claim descent from divine clan ancestors "only a degree less sublime than the imperial ancestors".Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334'', pp. 35–36. It is said that soon after the beginning of [[Jimmu]]'s reign, a Master of Ceremonies (''saishu'') was appointed; and this office was commonly held by a member of the Nakatomi clan after the 8th century.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 249 n10. This was due to the hereditary nature of both governmental positions and clan roles – a clan's role might be to supply warriors, or, in the case of the Nakatomi, to conduct [[Shinto]] rites and hold the associated positions. Though their material holdings were not the most extensive, their spiritual and ritual importance placed the Nakatomi and Imibe second only to the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House]] during their heyday. One particularly important ritual which the head of the Nakatomi clan oversaw was the ''Ōharai'' purification rite, performed twice every year, in which the High Priest (of the Nakatomi clan) asked the ''[[kami]]'' to cleanse the spirits of all of the people of their impurities. === Theories about their relationship with Takemikazuchi and the Ō clan === {{Main|Takemikazuchi|Ō clan}} According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},{{cite book |last=小向 |first=正司 |title=神道の本 |publisher=学研 |year=1992 |series=Books Esoterica |volume=2 |pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000) and was a god of maritime travel. However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}). The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.) When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty. ==Asuka period== As a result of the Nakatomis' ritual position and role in the [[Asuka period]], they were among the chief advocates of conservatism in the controversy over the introduction of [[Buddhism]] to Japan in the 6th century. However, by the time of [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]], in the early 7th century, the clan had switched sides, possibly as a result of their loyalty and close connection to the Imperial family; following [[Prince Shōtoku]], likely the most famous advocate of Buddhism in all of Japanese history, and later [[Emperor Tenji|Prince Naka no Ōe]], the Nakatomi helped eliminate the [[Soga clan]], powerful and very active supporters of Buddhism, and of the current administration of the time (see [[Isshi Incident]]). The clan soon came to be opposed by a number of other clans which vied for power and prestige at Court, and for influence over the Imperial succession. It is said however, that despite being overshadowed by others in terms of pure material wealth, the head of the Nakatomi clan was, in the mid-7th century, the most powerful man in Japan. Even into the 8th century, members of the Nakatomi clan maintained their important ritual position, becoming hereditary heads of the ''[[Jingi-kan]]'' (Department of Rites) established by the [[Code of Taihō]] in 701. == Nakatomi clan after Fujiwara no Kamatari == Arguably the most well-known clan leader, [[Nakatomi no Kamatari]] was granted the name [[Fujiwara]] by [[Emperor Tenji]] as a reward for loyal service to the sovereign. Kamatari is honored as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan]], which accumulated extraordinary powers and prestige in the [[Heian period]] (794–1185). Only the descendants of Fujiwara no Kamatari's eldest son Fuhito were allowed the name of Fujiwara. All the other members of the Nakatomi clan kept their original family name until [[Nakatomi no Imimaro]] was promoted to ''[[chūnagon]]'' with the support of Fuhito. In 764, Kiyomaro, the son of Imimaro, sided with [[Empress Kōken]] during the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]], and in 769, he was granted the name Ōnakatomi, thus establishing the [[Ōnakatomi clan]]. In the [[Heian period]], all three legitimate houses of Nakatomi clan were granted the name Ōnakatomi. [[Nakatomi no Ichishi]] was the last ''jingihaku'' (Head of the Department of Worship) of the Nakatomi clan, and his son [[Ōnakatomi no Itohito|Itohito]] later took the name of Ōnakatomi, thus being the last member of the Nakatomi clan.{{Cite book|last=Ota|first=Akira|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten, Volume 1 (姓氏家系大辞典、第1巻)|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1934|pages=1257–1261, 大中臣|language=Japanese}} ==Nakatomi Family Tree (大中臣系図)== '''Ikatsu ōmi-no-mikoto''' (雷大臣命) 　┃ '''O-o-obase-no-mikoto''' (大小橋命) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Amahisa-no-kimi''' (中臣阿麻毘舎卿) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Abiko''' (中臣阿毘古) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Mahito''' (中臣真人) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kamako''' (中臣鎌子) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Kuroda''' (中臣黒田) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Tokiwa''' (中臣常磐) 　┃ '''Nakatomi no Katanoko''' (中臣可多能祜) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''Nakatomi no Mikeko''' (中臣御食子) '''Nakatomi no Kuniko''' (中臣国子) '''Nakatomi no Nukateko''' (中臣糠手子) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　┃ 　┃ Second Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏二門) Third Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏三門) 　┣━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ '''[[Fujiwara no Kamatari]]''' (藤原鎌足, 614–669) '''Nakatomi no Hisata''' (中臣久多) '''Nakatomi no Tareme''' (中臣垂目) 　┃　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　 　 　┃ [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) First Branch of Nakatomi clan (中臣氏一門) ==See also== *[[Kogo Shūi]]—a record of the conflict between the Nakatomi and Inbe clans. ==References== {{reflist|1}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Aristocracy of Classical Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakatomi clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia clan]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakatomi clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakatomi
